definition: A hypothesized signal linking correlation regime to equity factor rotation: high-accruals (Q5) outperformed low-accruals (Q1) by ~15pp in 2020-21 (negative correlation regime); reversed to Q1 outperforming Q5 by ~12pp in 2022-23 (positive correlation). The mechanism (discount rate volatility exposing low-quality earnings) is logically sound but represents an n=1 observation.
evidence: Q5 (high accruals) outperformed Q1 (low accruals) by ~15pp in 2020-21
Reversed to Q1 outperforming Q5 by ~12pp in 2022-23
Mechanism: discount rate volatility under positive correlation exposes low-quality earnings
Debate pair_1 identified n=1 problem as 'disqualifying for the strength of the claim'
open_questions: Can additional historical episodes validate the signal?
Is the factor rotation driven by correlation regime per se, or by the underlying rate volatility that accompanies regime shifts?
Would a sector-neutral version of the signal be more robust?
